medical_history.please_briefly_describe_your_health:
Over the past two years I have been dealing with persistent lower back pain that radiates into my left buttock and occasionally down the back of my left leg to about the knee. The pain started gradually after years of working as a software engineer, sitting long hours at a desk, and worsened significantly after I slipped while lifting a heavy box in my garage in late 2022. Since then, I experience a constant dull ache in my lower back (I would rate it around 3–4/10 most days), with sharp, shooting pain (up to 7–8/10) when I bend forward, sit longer than about 30–40 minutes, or lift anything heavier than about 10–15 pounds.

Mornings are usually the stiffest—getting out of bed takes me a few minutes of slow movement and stretching before I can stand up straight. Prolonged sitting at my computer, long car rides, and standing in one spot (like cooking dinner) all tend to aggravate the symptoms. Walking at a moderate pace for 20–30 minutes and doing gentle stretching helps reduce the pain somewhat. I also notice that stress and poor sleep make the pain feel worse and more widespread.

Functionally, I can still take care of myself (dressing, bathing, toileting, etc.) and I am able to work full time, but I have had to modify how I do many tasks. At work, I now use a sit–stand desk and a lumbar support cushion and try to take short standing or walking breaks every 30 minutes. At home, I avoid lifting my 7-year-old daughter, doing heavy yard work, or carrying heavy grocery bags. I can climb stairs, but I prefer to use handrails and take them slowly one step at a time when I am having a flare-up.

In addition to the back pain, I have mild, well-controlled hypertension and slightly elevated cholesterol. I take medication for both and see my primary care physician regularly. My general fitness level is moderate: I walk 3–4 times a week and occasionally ride a stationary bike, but I have avoided more vigorous exercise out of fear of worsening my back. I do not have any known heart or lung problems, and I am able to walk several city blocks and climb one to two flights of stairs without needing to stop due to shortness of breath.

My main goals for physiotherapy are to reduce the frequency and intensity of the back and leg pain, improve my core strength and posture, and regain confidence in bending, lifting, and playing more actively with my children. I would also like guidance on safe exercises, ergonomic adjustments, and strategies to prevent further flare-ups so that I can continue working in my job without feeling constantly worried about my back.
medical_history.please_list_your_underlying_illness_and_:
Underlying illnesses:
- Hypertension (high blood pressure), diagnosed 2016 – currently treated with lisinopril 10 mg once daily. Blood pressure typically around 125–130/80–85 mmHg at recent visits.
- Hyperlipidemia (elevated cholesterol), diagnosed 2019 – treated with atorvastatin 20 mg once daily. Last lipid panel in 2024 showed improved levels per my primary care physician.
- Seasonal allergic rhinitis (pollen allergy) since adolescence – managed with over-the-counter antihistamines as needed.

Accidents and injuries:
- 2001 – Mild concussion from a bicycle fall (no loss of consciousness >1–2 minutes, no surgery, recovered fully with rest).
- 2010 – Left ankle sprain while playing recreational basketball; treated conservatively with rest, ice, compression, elevation, and short-term physiotherapy. No fracture.
- November 2022 – Acute low back strain while lifting a heavy box awkwardly in my garage. This incident triggered the persistent low back and left-sided sciatic pain that I am currently seeking rehabilitation for. I did not have surgery but was treated with oral anti-inflammatory medications, a short course of muscle relaxants, and a brief period of rest followed by basic home exercises.

Surgeries:
- 1998 – Appendectomy (removal of appendix) due to acute appendicitis. Uncomplicated recovery.
- 2014 – Right inguinal hernia repair with mesh. Completed standard post-operative physiotherapy instructions; no chronic pain or issues from the surgery.

No history of spine surgery, joint replacements, cardiac surgery, or major traumatic injuries such as fractures requiring surgical fixation.
medical_history.co_existing_diseases_eg_heart_and_kidney: Mild, well-controlled hypertension (diagnosed 2016) and hyperlipidemia (diagnosed 2019) as described above. No history of coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, stroke, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, chronic liver disease, autoimmune disease, or cancer. No known respiratory diseases such as asthma or COPD. Normal kidney and liver function on blood tests according to my primary care physician as of mid-2024.
medical_history.metal_body_parts_valve_pacemaker_stent_i: Right inguinal hernia repair mesh placed in 2014 (non-removable surgical mesh in groin area). No pacemaker, no heart valve prosthesis, no vascular stents, no joint replacements, and no metal screws or plates in the spine or extremities as far as I am aware.

rehabilitation_plan.rehabilitation_will_be_performed_others_: This rehabilitation stay is planned as a continuation and intensification of previous outpatient physiotherapy for chronic low back pain with left-sided radicular symptoms. I completed an 8-week course of outpatient physiotherapy from March to May 2024, which focused on basic core stabilization, gentle stretching, and education on posture and ergonomics. While that helped somewhat, I still experience daily pain and functional limitations. My referring physician and current physiotherapist recommended a more comprehensive, concentrated rehabilitation program at Columna Medica to further address strength, mobility, and pain management. There is no surgery currently scheduled or planned; the aim is conservative management and prevention of future deterioration that might otherwise lead to surgical consideration.

functional_status_and_accommodation_need.need_help_with_activities_yes: I am generally independent with all basic activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, feeding myself, toileting, and moving around my home). However, I do occasionally ask my wife or children for help with heavier or more awkward tasks, such as carrying large laundry baskets up and down the stairs, lifting heavy grocery bags from the car, or moving furniture. I also sometimes ask my wife to help with tasks that require prolonged bending, like cleaning lower kitchen cabinets or picking up multiple objects from the floor, especially during pain flare-ups. I do not currently use any assistive devices such as a cane, walker, or wheelchair, and I can manage stairs slowly with the handrail. I am able to drive myself, though I avoid long drives without breaks. Overall, I do not require formal daily living assistance, but I self-limit and delegate heavier physical tasks to family members to prevent worsening my back pain.
